questions.question: A provisional psychologist realises they forgot to update the file after a complex session involving a boundary concern and a treatment change. Their supervisor is off-site and the client is not due back for two weeks. What is the best action?

options.A: Leave the note out because the client will not remember the details anyway.
options.B: Complete an accurate contemporaneous note and flag the issue for supervision.
options.C: Delete the session plan so the record looks cleaner.
options.D: Write a vague note later so the boundary issue is less obvious.
options.E: Wait until the next supervision meeting before updating the record.

questions.correct_explanation: Record keeping should be accurate and timely, especially when the session involved a clinical change or boundary issue.

distractor_explanations.A: Omitting relevant information weakens continuity of care and accountability.
distractor_explanations.C: Deleting records is inappropriate and compromises integrity.
distractor_explanations.D: Vagueness can be misleading and does not meet documentation standards.
distractor_explanations.E: Delaying updates risks loss of detail and weakens the clinical record.

citations: APS Code of Ethics (2007, amended 2023), Principle C.1 — Integrity
Psychology Board of Australia Code of Conduct (2020), Section 6 — Clinical records

questions.question: In a community service, a 19-year-old client says they do not want their parent informed about session content. The parent later requests an update because they are paying for treatment. The client has not consented to any disclosure. What should the psychologist do next?

options.A: Share the notes but leave out the most sensitive details.
options.B: Decline disclosure, explain confidentiality limits, and seek supervision if needed.
options.C: Provide a brief summary because the parent is funding therapy.
options.D: Ask the parent to sign a form and then disclose the information.
options.E: Give a verbal family update because it is routine practice.

questions.correct_explanation: Funding treatment does not override confidentiality. The safest response is to decline disclosure without consent and clarify the limits of privacy with the client.

distractor_explanations.A: Partial disclosure is still disclosure if there is no consent or legal basis.
distractor_explanations.C: Payment for treatment does not create a right to private clinical information.
distractor_explanations.D: A form signed by the parent alone does not authorise disclosure.
distractor_explanations.E: Routine updates still require client consent and attention to privacy limits.

citations: Psychology Board of Australia Code of Conduct (2020), Section 5 — Confidentiality
Privacy Act 1988 (Cth), Australian Privacy Principle 6

questions.question: During a regional telehealth session, a client says they are alone, distressed, and do not want to continue, but the psychologist cannot confirm their location. What should the psychologist prioritise?

options.A: End the call immediately and wait for the client to reconnect later.
options.B: Continue as normal because the client consented to telehealth.
options.C: Pause treatment content, verify location, and follow the telehealth emergency plan.
options.D: Ask a family member to join without speaking with the client first.
options.E: Switch to text messaging only and keep the session informal.

questions.correct_explanation: Telehealth requires an emergency plan and active location verification when risk emerges. Safety, not continuation of content, is the priority.

distractor_explanations.A: Ending without a plan may leave the client unsupported.
distractor_explanations.B: Telehealth consent does not remove the duty to manage emerging risk.
distractor_explanations.D: Bringing others in without consent can damage trust unless there is urgent necessity.
distractor_explanations.E: Text-only communication is not an appropriate risk-management substitute.

citations: Psychology Board of Australia Code of Conduct (2020), Section 2 — Person-centred practice
AHPRA Guidelines for registered health practitioners (2014)

questions.question: A provisional psychologist in a community health service notices a client has become increasingly hopeless and mentioned giving away possessions. The client ends the session before a formal risk review can be completed. What is the most appropriate next step?

options.A: Initiate urgent follow-up using the clinic risk pathway and supervisor review.
options.B: Wait until the next booked session so the client can settle.
options.C: Send a text asking the client to confirm they are safe and stop there.
options.D: Contact the police immediately without any further assessment.
options.E: Document the presentation and assume the client will rebook if needed.

questions.correct_explanation: Hopelessness plus possible preparatory behaviour requires active follow-up. A provisional psychologist should use the risk pathway, seek supervision, and take reasonable steps promptly.

distractor_explanations.B: Delaying follow-up may miss a potentially acute risk window.
distractor_explanations.C: A text alone is not an adequate risk management response.
distractor_explanations.D: Police contact is not the automatic first step unless immediate danger is clearly present.
distractor_explanations.E: Documentation is necessary but not a substitute for active risk management.

citations: APS Code of Ethics (2007, amended 2023), Principle B.1 — Propriety
Psychology Board of Australia Code of Conduct (2020), Section 4 — Providing good care

questions.question: During a after-hours video session, a client says they are alone, distressed, and do not want to continue, but the psychologist cannot confirm their location. What should the psychologist prioritise?

options.A: End the call immediately and wait for the client to reconnect later.
options.B: Switch to text messaging only and keep the session informal.
options.C: Pause treatment content, verify location, and follow the telehealth emergency plan.
options.D: Ask a family member to join without speaking with the client first.
options.E: Continue as normal because the client consented to telehealth.

questions.correct_explanation: Telehealth requires an emergency plan and active location verification when risk emerges. Safety, not continuation of content, is the priority.

distractor_explanations.A: Ending without a plan may leave the client unsupported.
distractor_explanations.B: Text-only communication is not an appropriate risk-management substitute.
distractor_explanations.D: Bringing others in without consent can damage trust unless there is urgent necessity.

questions.question: A provisional psychologist realises they forgot to update the file after a complex session involving a boundary concern and a treatment change. Their supervisor is off-site and the client is not due back for two weeks. What is the best action?

options.A: Complete an accurate contemporaneous note and flag the issue for supervision.
options.B: Wait until the next supervision meeting before updating the record.
options.C: Write a vague note later so the boundary issue is less obvious.
options.D: Delete the session plan so the record looks cleaner.
options.E: Leave the note out because the client will not remember the details anyway.

questions.correct_explanation: Record keeping should be accurate and timely, especially when the session involved a clinical change or boundary issue.

distractor_explanations.B: Delaying updates risks loss of detail and weakens the clinical record.
distractor_explanations.C: Vagueness can be misleading and does not meet documentation standards.
distractor_explanations.D: Deleting records is inappropriate and compromises integrity.
distractor_explanations.E: Omitting relevant information weakens continuity of care and accountability.

citations: APS Code of Ethics (2007, amended 2023), Principle C.1 — Integrity

questions.question: A recently arrived refugee client has limited English and asks whether they can bring an interpreter to a cognitive screening appointment. The psychologist is worried this could affect test validity. What is the best response?

options.A: Refuse the interpreter so the test results remain fully standardised.
options.B: Use family members as interpreters to reduce the risk of misunderstanding.
options.C: Proceed in English only because that is how the instrument was normed.
options.D: Use a qualified interpreter where needed and choose instruments appropriate to the client's language background.
options.E: Delay the assessment until the client becomes fluent in English.

questions.correct_explanation: Assessment should be fair and accessible, which may require an interpreter and cautious test selection.

distractor_explanations.A: Excluding interpretation can make the assessment invalid or unfair.
distractor_explanations.B: Family interpreters can compromise accuracy, privacy, and clinical neutrality.
distractor_explanations.C: English-only testing may be inappropriate when language barriers are material.
distractor_explanations.E: Assessment should not be postponed unnecessarily if suitable adaptations are available.

citations: APS Code of Ethics (2007, amended 2023), Principle A.3 — Informed consent
APS Guidelines for working with people who have intellectual disabilities

questions.question: A 27-year-old client describes poor sleep, low mood, and says they feel like a burden. They deny a current plan but admit having thought about not waking up. Which assessment action is most appropriate next?

options.A: Focus only on depression severity and avoid asking more about suicide.
options.B: Conduct a structured suicide risk assessment including intent, means, and protective factors.
options.C: Tell the client to contact emergency services if their thoughts worsen and end the session.
options.D: Document the symptoms and schedule the next routine appointment.
options.E: Refer the client elsewhere without discussing risk in the current session.

questions.correct_explanation: Suicidal ideation requires a structured risk assessment, even when there is no current plan.

distractor_explanations.A: Avoiding direct suicide questions can miss important risk details.
distractor_explanations.C: Advice may be part of management, but it is not a substitute for assessment.
distractor_explanations.D: Routine scheduling without risk assessment is insufficient.
distractor_explanations.E: Referral alone does not discharge the current duty of care.

citations: Psychology Board of Australia Code of Conduct (2020), Section 4 — Providing good care
AHPRA Guidelines for registered health practitioners (2014)

questions.question: A client reports that they have a firearm at home, have been drinking heavily, and are angry after a relationship breakup. They say they are "not sure what I might do tonight". What is the priority?

options.A: Focus on substance use first and ignore the weapon access until later.
options.B: Assume the client is only venting because they have not named a specific target.
options.C: Conduct immediate high-risk management, including emergency escalation and supervision.
options.D: Take a note and reassess at the next session if the client survives the night.
options.E: End the session once the client says they do not want hospitalisation.

questions.correct_explanation: Access to means, intoxication, and uncertainty about imminent behaviour converge to create an acute high-risk presentation.

distractor_explanations.A: Substance use may be relevant, but it is not the immediate priority in this scenario.
distractor_explanations.B: The absence of a named target does not reduce the seriousness of the threat.
distractor_explanations.D: Delaying action is unsafe when the risk is immediate.
distractor_explanations.E: Client refusal does not remove the clinician's duty to respond to imminent risk.

citations: Psychology Board of Australia Code of Conduct (2020), Section 4 — Providing good care
Health Practitioner Regulation National Law, Section 140 — Mandatory notifications

questions.question: A 15-year-old is referred for learning and attention concerns. The parent says the school is exaggerating the issue, while the teacher reports significant behaviour problems and missed assignments. What should guide the assessment plan?

options.A: Conclude it is behavioural rather than cognitive based on the school report alone.
options.B: Gather collateral information from multiple sources and interpret results in context.
options.C: Use only a self-report symptom checklist to avoid bias from the school.
options.D: Rely on the parent report because they are the legal guardian.
options.E: Delay testing until all informants agree on the reason for referral.

questions.correct_explanation: A balanced assessment should integrate multiple sources because single-informant data can be biased or incomplete.

distractor_explanations.A: A school report alone is not sufficient to conclude the underlying cause.
distractor_explanations.C: A single self-report tool is not enough for a complex developmental concern.
distractor_explanations.D: Legal guardianship does not make one informant sufficient for a fair assessment.
distractor_explanations.E: Perfect agreement is not required before proceeding with assessment.

citations: APS Code of Ethics (2007, amended 2023), Principle A.4 — Justice
Psychology Board of Australia Code of Conduct (2020), Section 6 — Clinical records

questions.question: A recently arrived refugee client has limited English and asks whether they can bring an interpreter to a cognitive screening appointment. The psychologist is worried this could affect test validity. What is the best response?

options.A: Refuse the interpreter so the test results remain fully standardised.
options.B: Use a qualified interpreter where needed and choose instruments appropriate to the client's language background.
options.C: Use family members as interpreters to reduce the risk of misunderstanding.
options.D: Proceed in English only because that is how the instrument was normed.
options.E: Delay the assessment until the client becomes fluent in English.

questions.correct_explanation: Assessment should be fair and accessible, which may require an interpreter and cautious test selection.

distractor_explanations.A: Excluding interpretation can make the assessment invalid or unfair.
distractor_explanations.C: Family interpreters can compromise accuracy, privacy, and clinical neutrality.
distractor_explanations.D: English-only testing may be inappropriate when language barriers are material.
distractor_explanations.E: Assessment should not be postponed unnecessarily if suitable adaptations are available.

citations: APS Code of Ethics (2007, amended 2023), Principle A.3 — Informed consent
APS Guidelines for working with people who have intellectual disabilities

questions.question: A 15-year-old is referred for learning and attention concerns. The parent says the school is exaggerating the issue, while the teacher reports significant behaviour problems and missed assignments. What should guide the assessment plan?

options.A: Gather collateral information from multiple sources and interpret results in context.
options.B: Use only a self-report symptom checklist to avoid bias from the school.
options.C: Rely on the parent report because they are the legal guardian.
options.D: Conclude it is behavioural rather than cognitive based on the school report alone.
options.E: Delay testing until all informants agree on the reason for referral.

questions.correct_explanation: A balanced assessment should integrate multiple sources because single-informant data can be biased or incomplete.

distractor_explanations.B: A single self-report tool is not enough for a complex developmental concern.
distractor_explanations.C: Legal guardianship does not make one informant sufficient for a fair assessment.
distractor_explanations.D: A school report alone is not sufficient to conclude the underlying cause.
distractor_explanations.E: Perfect agreement is not required before proceeding with assessment.

citations: APS Code of Ethics (2007, amended 2023), Principle A.4 — Justice
Psychology Board of Australia Code of Conduct (2020), Section 6 — Clinical records

questions.question: A 27-year-old client describes poor sleep, low mood, and says they feel like a burden. They deny a current plan but admit having thought about not waking up. Which assessment action is most appropriate next?

options.A: Tell the client to contact emergency services if their thoughts worsen and end the session.
options.B: Focus only on depression severity and avoid asking more about suicide.
options.C: Document the symptoms and schedule the next routine appointment.
options.D: Conduct a structured suicide risk assessment including intent, means, and protective factors.
options.E: Refer the client elsewhere without discussing risk in the current session.

questions.correct_explanation: Suicidal ideation requires a structured risk assessment, even when there is no current plan.

distractor_explanations.A: Advice may be part of management, but it is not a substitute for assessment.
distractor_explanations.B: Avoiding direct suicide questions can miss important risk details.
distractor_explanations.C: Routine scheduling without risk assessment is insufficient.
distractor_explanations.E: Referral alone does not discharge the current duty of care.

citations: Psychology Board of Australia Code of Conduct (2020), Section 4 — Providing good care
AHPRA Guidelines for registered health practitioners (2014)

questions.question: A client reports that they have a firearm at home, have been drinking heavily, and are angry after a relationship breakup. They say they are "not sure what I might do tonight". What is the priority?

options.A: Assume the client is only venting because they have not named a specific target.
options.B: Focus on substance use first and ignore the weapon access until later.
options.C: Take a note and reassess at the next session if the client survives the night.
options.D: Conduct immediate high-risk management, including emergency escalation and supervision.
options.E: End the session once the client says they do not want hospitalisation.

Health Practitioner Regulation National Law, Section 140 — Mandatory notifications

questions.question: A client reports that they have a firearm at home, have been drinking heavily, and are angry after a relationship breakup. They say they are "not sure what I might do tonight". What is the priority?

options.A: Conduct immediate high-risk management, including emergency escalation and supervision.
options.B: Take a note and reassess at the next session if the client survives the night.
options.C: Focus on substance use first and ignore the weapon access until later.
options.D: Assume the client is only venting because they have not named a specific target.
options.E: End the session once the client says they do not want hospitalisation.

questions.correct_explanation: Access to means, intoxication, and uncertainty about imminent behaviour converge to create an acute high-risk presentation.

distractor_explanations.B: Delaying action is unsafe when the risk is immediate.
distractor_explanations.C: Substance use may be relevant, but it is not the immediate priority in this scenario.
distractor_explanations.D: The absence of a named target does not reduce the seriousness of the threat.
distractor_explanations.E: Client refusal does not remove the clinician's duty to respond to imminent risk.

citations: Psychology Board of Australia Code of Conduct (2020), Section 4 — Providing good care
Health Practitioner Regulation National Law, Section 140 — Mandatory notifications

questions.question: A 15-year-old is referred for learning and attention concerns. The parent says the school is exaggerating the issue, while the teacher reports significant behaviour problems and missed assignments. What should guide the assessment plan?

options.A: Gather collateral information from multiple sources and interpret results in context.
options.B: Use only a self-report symptom checklist to avoid bias from the school.
options.C: Rely on the parent report because they are the legal guardian.
options.D: Conclude it is behavioural rather than cognitive based on the school report alone.
options.E: Delay testing until all informants agree on the reason for referral.

questions.correct_explanation: A balanced assessment should integrate multiple sources because single-informant data can be biased or incomplete.

distractor_explanations.B: A single self-report tool is not enough for a complex developmental concern.
distractor_explanations.C: Legal guardianship does not make one informant sufficient for a fair assessment.
distractor_explanations.D: A school report alone is not sufficient to conclude the underlying cause.
distractor_explanations.E: Perfect agreement is not required before proceeding with assessment.

citations: APS Code of Ethics (2007, amended 2023), Principle A.4 — Justice
Psychology Board of Australia Code of Conduct (2020), Section 6 — Clinical records

questions.question: A client with panic symptoms wants a psychologist to "just tell me what to do" and expects reassurance during every anxious spike. Which intervention choice best fits an evidence-based CBT approach?

options.A: Use psychoeducation, monitoring, and graduated exposure with collaboratively set goals.
options.B: Change the topic whenever the client reports bodily symptoms.
options.C: Tell the client to think positively whenever anxiety appears.
options.D: Focus only on childhood causes before teaching any coping skills.
options.E: Provide reassurance at every session and avoid homework to keep the client calm.

questions.correct_explanation: CBT for panic commonly uses psychoeducation, symptom monitoring, and graded exposure.

distractor_explanations.B: Avoiding symptom discussion undermines treatment and learning.
distractor_explanations.C: Positive thinking alone is not a full CBT intervention.
distractor_explanations.D: Historical factors can matter, but intervention should still be active and skills-based.
distractor_explanations.E: Constant reassurance can reinforce avoidance and dependence.

citations: Psychology Board of Australia Code of Conduct (2020), Section 4 — Providing good care
Beck et al. (1979), Cognitive Therapy of Depression

questions.question: A client with a trauma history freezes and goes silent when asked to describe a recent assault. The psychologist wants a full timeline for treatment planning. What is the best approach?

options.A: Slow the pace, stabilise, and use trauma-informed choice and grounding.
options.B: Terminate the session because the client is not ready for therapy.
options.C: Keep asking the same question until the client answers clearly.
options.D: Switch to confrontation because silence is non-compliance.
options.E: Push for details so the client learns not to avoid the memory.

questions.correct_explanation: A trauma-informed response prioritises emotional safety, choice, and pacing.

distractor_explanations.B: A trauma response does not mean treatment must stop entirely.
distractor_explanations.C: Repetition without attunement can intensify freezing and harm rapport.
distractor_explanations.D: Confrontation is likely to escalate shame or shutdown.
distractor_explanations.E: Forcing disclosure can increase distress and reduce safety.

citations: Psychology Board of Australia Code of Conduct (2020), Section 4 — Providing good care
AHPRA Guidelines for registered health practitioners (2014)

questions.question: A client becomes annoyed after the psychologist gently challenges an avoidance pattern and says the session felt "judgy". The client is otherwise engaged and wants to keep attending. What is the best next step?

options.A: Acknowledge the rupture, explore the impact, and repair collaboratively.
options.B: End therapy because the client is being resistant.
options.C: Apologise only if the client explicitly asks for it.
options.D: Ignore the comment and move straight to the next agenda item.
options.E: Defend the intervention because the psychologist is the expert.

questions.correct_explanation: Therapeutic rupture repair is part of good care and helps preserve the alliance.

distractor_explanations.B: A single rupture does not justify dropping a willing client.
distractor_explanations.C: Repair often needs direct discussion, not just passive apology.
distractor_explanations.D: Ignoring the issue can leave the alliance damaged.
distractor_explanations.E: Defensiveness usually worsens the rupture.

citations: Psychology Board of Australia Code of Conduct (2020), Section 2 — Person-centred practice
APS Code of Ethics (2007, amended 2023), Principle B.1 — Propriety

questions.question: A provisional psychologist is co-facilitating a skills group. One participant starts giving detailed advice about another participant's personal situation. What is the best facilitator response?

options.A: Ask the participant to leave without any explanation.
options.B: Ignore it because the group will self-correct over time.
options.C: Redirect to group rules, protect boundaries, and reinforce respectful participation.
options.D: Share the other participant's notes so the group can understand the context.
options.E: Allow the discussion because peer support is always therapeutic.

questions.correct_explanation: Facilitators should maintain a safe and respectful group frame and reinforce boundaries.

distractor_explanations.A: Removal may be disproportionate unless the disruption is serious and repeated.
distractor_explanations.B: Ignoring boundary issues can destabilise the group.
distractor_explanations.D: Sharing another member's notes breaches privacy and trust.
distractor_explanations.E: Peer support is useful, but uncontained advice can become harmful.

citations: Psychology Board of Australia Code of Conduct (2020), Section 7 — Collaboration and teamwork
APS Code of Ethics (2007, amended 2023), Principle A.1 — General Respect

options.A: Slow the pace, stabilise, and use trauma-informed choice and grounding.
options.B: Push for details so the client learns not to avoid the memory.
options.C: Keep asking the same question until the client answers clearly.
options.D: Terminate the session because the client is not ready for therapy.
options.E: Switch to confrontation because silence is non-compliance.

questions.correct_explanation: A trauma-informed response prioritises emotional safety, choice, and pacing.

distractor_explanations.B: Forcing disclosure can increase distress and reduce safety.
distractor_explanations.C: Repetition without attunement can intensify freezing and harm rapport.
distractor_explanations.D: A trauma response does not mean treatment must stop entirely.
distractor_explanations.E: Confrontation is likely to escalate shame or shutdown.

citations: Psychology Board of Australia Code of Conduct (2020), Section 4 — Providing good care
AHPRA Guidelines for registered health practitioners (2014)

questions.question: A client becomes annoyed after the psychologist gently challenges an avoidance pattern and says the session felt "judgy". The client is otherwise engaged and wants to keep attending. What is the best next step?

options.A: Acknowledge the rupture, explore the impact, and repair collaboratively.
options.B: Defend the intervention because the psychologist is the expert.
options.C: Apologise only if the client explicitly asks for it.
options.D: Ignore the comment and move straight to the next agenda item.
options.E: End therapy because the client is being resistant.

questions.correct_explanation: Therapeutic rupture repair is part of good care and helps preserve the alliance.

distractor_explanations.B: Defensiveness usually worsens the rupture.
distractor_explanations.C: Repair often needs direct discussion, not just passive apology.
distractor_explanations.D: Ignoring the issue can leave the alliance damaged.
distractor_explanations.E: A single rupture does not justify dropping a willing client.

citations: Psychology Board of Australia Code of Conduct (2020), Section 2 — Person-centred practice
APS Code of Ethics (2007, amended 2023), Principle B.1 — Propriety

questions.question: A provisional psychologist is co-facilitating a skills group. One participant starts giving detailed advice about another participant's personal situation. What is the best facilitator response?

options.A: Share the other participant's notes so the group can understand the context.
options.B: Ignore it because the group will self-correct over time.
options.C: Allow the discussion because peer support is always therapeutic.
options.D: Redirect to group rules, protect boundaries, and reinforce respectful participation.
options.E: Ask the participant to leave without any explanation.

questions.correct_explanation: Facilitators should maintain a safe and respectful group frame and reinforce boundaries.

distractor_explanations.A: Sharing another member's notes breaches privacy and trust.
distractor_explanations.B: Ignoring boundary issues can destabilise the group.
distractor_explanations.C: Peer support is useful, but uncontained advice can become harmful.
distractor_explanations.E: Removal may be disproportionate unless the disruption is serious and repeated.

questions.question: A client with panic symptoms wants a psychologist to "just tell me what to do" and expects reassurance during every anxious spike. Which intervention choice best fits an evidence-based CBT approach?

options.A: Change the topic whenever the client reports bodily symptoms.
options.B: Tell the client to think positively whenever anxiety appears.
options.C: Use psychoeducation, monitoring, and graduated exposure with collaboratively set goals.
options.D: Provide reassurance at every session and avoid homework to keep the client calm.
options.E: Focus only on childhood causes before teaching any coping skills.

questions.correct_explanation: CBT for panic commonly uses psychoeducation, symptom monitoring, and graded exposure.

distractor_explanations.A: Avoiding symptom discussion undermines treatment and learning.
distractor_explanations.B: Positive thinking alone is not a full CBT intervention.
distractor_explanations.D: Constant reassurance can reinforce avoidance and dependence.
distractor_explanations.E: Historical factors can matter, but intervention should still be active and skills-based.

citations: Psychology Board of Australia Code of Conduct (2020), Section 4 — Providing good care
Beck et al. (1979), Cognitive Therapy of Depression

questions.question: A provisional psychologist is drafting a report for a referrer. The client asked that a sensitive family issue not be mentioned, but it is relevant to the referral question. What is the most appropriate approach?

options.A: Omit the issue entirely so the client feels protected.
options.B: Send the raw notes instead of a formal report.
options.C: Include only what is necessary, within the consent limits discussed with the client.
options.D: Use vague language that hides the key information.
options.E: Include everything in full detail because the referrer paid for the report.

questions.correct_explanation: Reports should be relevant, proportionate, and consistent with consent and confidentiality limits.

distractor_explanations.A: Omitting relevant material can make the report misleading.
distractor_explanations.B: Raw notes are not a substitute for a considered report.
distractor_explanations.D: Vagueness can reduce clinical usefulness and accuracy.
distractor_explanations.E: Reports should not include unnecessary sensitive detail.

citations: Psychology Board of Australia Code of Conduct (2020), Section 6 — Clinical records
AHPRA Guidelines for registered health practitioners (2014)

questions.question: A psychologist receives a subpoena for records and is unsure whether they can refuse because the notes contain sensitive third-party information. What is the most appropriate first step?

options.A: Release everything immediately without checking the scope.
options.B: Refuse to comply because third-party material is always protected.
options.C: Destroy the notes before the hearing date.
options.D: Seek legal/supervisory advice and review what must be disclosed under the order.
options.E: Ignore the subpoena unless the client asks about it.

questions.correct_explanation: A subpoena requires careful review of scope and legal obligations.

distractor_explanations.A: Disclosure should be limited to the scope of the order and lawful exceptions.
distractor_explanations.B: Third-party material may need careful handling, but it does not automatically nullify the subpoena.
distractor_explanations.C: Destroying records is inappropriate and potentially unlawful.
distractor_explanations.E: Ignoring a subpoena can create legal risk.

citations: Psychology Board of Australia Code of Conduct (2020), Section 8 — Conflicts, financial and other business dealings
APS Code of Ethics (2007, amended 2023), Principle C.1 — Integrity

questions.question: A psychologist is explaining assessment results to a client who prefers to speak through an interpreter. The client is visibly uncomfortable when family members offer to translate instead. What is the best choice?

options.A: Give the client a written summary only and end the session early.
options.B: Skip the interpreter and speak more slowly in English.
options.C: Ask the family to leave the room and proceed without communication support.
options.D: Use a qualified interpreter and maintain the client's privacy and control.
options.E: Use the family members because they know the client best.

questions.correct_explanation: Interpreter-assisted communication should prioritise accuracy, confidentiality, and client autonomy.

distractor_explanations.A: Written summaries alone may not be enough for nuanced feedback.
distractor_explanations.B: Slowing down English does not solve the language barrier.
distractor_explanations.C: Removing support without replacing it can leave the client unable to engage.
distractor_explanations.E: Family translators can compromise privacy and accuracy.

citations: APS Code of Ethics (2007, amended 2023), Principle A.2 — Propriety
AHPRA Guidelines for registered health practitioners (2014)

questions.question: A client's partner rings the clinic asking for an update because they are worried about recent mood changes. The client has not consented to sharing information with the partner. What should the psychologist do?

options.A: Confirm whether the partner is telling the truth before deciding.
options.B: Leave a voicemail with broad details so the partner feels reassured.
options.C: Do not disclose client information and explain the confidentiality process.
options.D: Share the client's diagnosis but not the treatment plan.
options.E: Give a general update because family concern is important.

questions.correct_explanation: Without consent or a lawful exception, information should not be disclosed to a partner.

distractor_explanations.A: Verifying the caller does not create permission to disclose.
distractor_explanations.B: Voicemail disclosure can still breach confidentiality.
distractor_explanations.D: Partial disclosure is still disclosure and needs a lawful basis.
distractor_explanations.E: Concern from a family member does not override privacy obligations.

citations: Psychology Board of Australia Code of Conduct (2020), Section 5 — Confidentiality
Privacy Act 1988 (Cth), Australian Privacy Principle 6

questions.question: A provisional psychologist is drafting a report for a referrer. The client asked that a sensitive family issue not be mentioned, but it is relevant to the referral question. What is the most appropriate approach?

options.A: Include everything in full detail because the referrer paid for the report.
options.B: Send the raw notes instead of a formal report.
options.C: Include only what is necessary, within the consent limits discussed with the client.
options.D: Omit the issue entirely so the client feels protected.
options.E: Use vague language that hides the key information.

questions.correct_explanation: Reports should be relevant, proportionate, and consistent with consent and confidentiality limits.

questions.question: A client's partner rings the clinic asking for an update because they are worried about recent mood changes. The client has not consented to sharing information with the partner. What should the psychologist do?

options.A: Give a general update because family concern is important.
options.B: Do not disclose client information and explain the confidentiality process.
options.C: Confirm whether the partner is telling the truth before deciding.
options.D: Share the client's diagnosis but not the treatment plan.
options.E: Leave a voicemail with broad details so the partner feels reassured.

questions.correct_explanation: Without consent or a lawful exception, information should not be disclosed to a partner.

distractor_explanations.C: Verifying the caller does not create permission to disclose.
distractor_explanations.D: Partial disclosure is still disclosure and needs a lawful basis.
distractor_explanations.E: Voicemail disclosure can still breach confidentiality.

citations: Psychology Board of Australia Code of Conduct (2020), Section 5 — Confidentiality
Privacy Act 1988 (Cth), Australian Privacy Principle 6

questions.question: A psychologist is explaining assessment results to a client who prefers to speak through an interpreter. The client is visibly uncomfortable when family members offer to translate instead. What is the best choice?

options.A: Skip the interpreter and speak more slowly in English.
options.B: Ask the family to leave the room and proceed without communication support.
options.C: Use the family members because they know the client best.
options.D: Use a qualified interpreter and maintain the client's privacy and control.
options.E: Give the client a written summary only and end the session early.

questions.correct_explanation: Interpreter-assisted communication should prioritise accuracy, confidentiality, and client autonomy.

distractor_explanations.A: Slowing down English does not solve the language barrier.
distractor_explanations.B: Removing support without replacing it can leave the client unable to engage.
distractor_explanations.C: Family translators can compromise privacy and accuracy.
distractor_explanations.E: Written summaries alone may not be enough for nuanced feedback.

citations: APS Code of Ethics (2007, amended 2023), Principle A.2 — Propriety
AHPRA Guidelines for registered health practitioners (2014)

questions.question: A psychologist receives a subpoena for records and is unsure whether they can refuse because the notes contain sensitive third-party information. What is the most appropriate first step?

options.A: Destroy the notes before the hearing date.
options.B: Seek legal/supervisory advice and review what must be disclosed under the order.
options.C: Refuse to comply because third-party material is always protected.
options.D: Ignore the subpoena unless the client asks about it.
options.E: Release everything immediately without checking the scope.

questions.correct_explanation: A subpoena requires careful review of scope and legal obligations.

distractor_explanations.A: Destroying records is inappropriate and potentially unlawful.
distractor_explanations.C: Third-party material may need careful handling, but it does not automatically nullify the subpoena.
distractor_explanations.D: Ignoring a subpoena can create legal risk.
distractor_explanations.E: Disclosure should be limited to the scope of the order and lawful exceptions.

citations: Psychology Board of Australia Code of Conduct (2020), Section 8 — Conflicts, financial and other business dealings
APS Code of Ethics (2007, amended 2023), Principle C.1 — Integrity
